company_profile.name: Amrize Ltd
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Amrize AG engages in the provision of various building solutions for infrastructure, commercial, and residential construction markets in North America. It operates through two segments, Building Materials and Building Envelope. The Building Materials segment offers cement and aggregates, as well as ready-mix concrete, asphalt, and other construction materials. The Building Envelope segment provides advanced roofing and wall systems, including single-ply membranes, insulation, shingles, sheathing, waterproofing, and protective coatings; and adhesives, tapes, and sealants. The company was formerly known as Holcim North America Finance Ltd and changed its name to Amrize AG in December 2013. Amrize AG was incorporated in 2023 and is based in Zug, Switzerland.
company_profile.description_summary:
Amrize provides building solutions across North America's infrastructure, commercial, and residential construction markets through two main segments. The Building Materials division supplies cement, aggregates, ready-mix concrete, and asphalt, while the Building Envelope segment offers roofing systems, insulation, waterproofing, and sealants. Together, these segments generate the company's primary revenue.

The company distinguishes itself by offering comprehensive, integrated building solutions spanning materials and envelope systems, serving the full spectrum of construction needs from foundational materials to protective finishing systems.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

company_profile.description: América Móvil, S.A.B. de C.V. provides telecommunications services in Latin America and internationally. It offers wireless and fixed-line voice services, including airtime, local, domestic, and international long-distance services; and network interconnection services. Additionally, it provides video, audio, and other media content through the internet directly from the content provider to the end user. It sells its products and services under the Telcel, Telmex Infinitum, and A1 brand names through a network of retailers and service centers to retail customers; and through sales force to corporate customers. The company was incorporated in 2000 and is based in Mexico City, Mexico.

company_profile.name: Amerant Bancorp Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
company_profile.description: Amerant Bancorp Inc. operates as the bank holding company for Amerant Bank, N.A. that provides banking products and services to individuals and businesses in the United States. The company offers checking, savings, business, and money market accounts; cash management services; and certificates of deposit. It also provides variable and fixed rate commercial real estate loans; owner-occupied; single-family residential; commercial; and Loans to financial institutions and acceptances, and consumer loans and overdrafts, such as automobile, personal, or loans secured by cash or securities and revolving credit card agreements. In addition, the company offers trust and estate planning products and services to high net worth customers; brokerage and investment advisory services in global capital markets; retail banking; mortgage; and wealth management and fiduciary services. Further, the company provides debit and credit cards, night depositories, direct deposits, cashier's checks, safe deposit boxes, letters of credit, wire transfer, remote deposit capture, and automated clearinghouse services; derivative instruments; and online and mobile banking, account balances, statements and other documents, online transfers and bill payment, and electronic delivery of customer statements services, as well as automated teller machines ATM, and banking by mobile devices, telephone, and mail. It also operates banking centers. The company was formerly known as Mercantil Bank Holding Corporation and changed its name to Amerant Bancorp Inc. in June 2019. Amerant Bancorp Inc. was founded in 1979 and is headquartered in Coral Gables, Florida.
company_profile.description_summary:
# Amerant Bancorp Inc. (AMTB)

Operates as a bank holding company providing comprehensive banking services to individuals and businesses across the United States. Revenue primarily derives from deposit accounts, commercial and residential real estate loans, consumer lending, and cash management services, alongside trust, wealth management, and brokerage offerings.

Distinguishes itself through specialized focus on high-net-worth clients, offering wealth management and investment advisory services in global capital markets alongside traditional retail banking operations.

_meta.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
_meta.method_explanation: Banks make money from the difference between what they charge on loans and what they pay on deposits — not from traditional business cash flows. Because of this, standard cash flow models (DCF) do not work well for banks. Instead, we focus on Price-to-Book (P/B), which compares the stock price to the value of the bank's assets on its books. Banks also carry a lot of debt by design — they borrow money to lend it out — so high leverage is normal and not a red flag.

quality.company_type_note: Banks and REITs carry structural leverage — do not describe financial_strength scores of 7+ as 'moderate concern' for these types.

Concentrating with industry-leading brands, the Company's hotel portfolio consists of 96 Marriott-branded hotels, 115 Hilton-branded hotels, five Hyatt-branded hotels and one independent hotel. Apple Hospitality REIT, Inc. was established on November 08, 2007 and incorporated in 2007 in Virginia, USA
company_profile.description_summary:
This publicly traded real estate investment trust owns and operates a diverse portfolio of upscale, rooms-focused hotels across the United States. With 217 hotels comprising approximately 29,600 guest rooms spread across 84 markets in 37 states and Washington, D.C., the company generates revenue primarily through hotel operations and guest accommodations.

Apple Hospitality distinguishes itself through partnerships with industry-leading hotel brands, including 96 Marriott-branded properties, 115 Hilton-branded properties, five Hyatt-branded properties, and one independent hotel, creating one of the largest and most diversified upscale hotel portfolios in the country.

_meta.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) own properties and are legally required to pay out most of their income as dividends. This means they rarely have much free cash flow left over, so DCF models give misleading results. Instead, we use Price-to-Book (P/B) to estimate the value of the underlying properties, and EV/EBITDA to compare the business to similar property companies.

company_profile.name: Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Asset manager/broker — DNI prioritised; standard CF metrics unreliable
company_profile.description: Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. is publicly owned investment manager. It provides its services to pension and profit sharing plans, trusts, endowments, foundations, charitable organizations, government entities, private funds and non-U.S. funds, as well as mutual funds, non-U.S. funds and collective trusts. It manages separate client-focused equity and fixed income portfolios. The firm invests in the public equity and fixed income markets across the globe. It invests in growth and value stocks of companies across all market capitalization. For fixed income component of its portfolio the firm invests in non-investment grade corporate bonds and secured and unsecured loans. It employs fundamental analysis to create its portfolios. Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. was founded in 1994 and is based in Milwaukee, Wisconsin with additional offices in Atlanta, Georgia; New York City; San Francisco, California; Leawood, Kansas; and London, United Kingdom.
company_profile.description_summary:
This publicly owned investment manager provides asset management services across equities and fixed income to institutional clients including pension plans, endowments, and foundations, as well as mutual funds and collective trusts. It generates revenue by managing separate client-focused portfolios invested globally in public markets, employing fundamental analysis to construct positions across growth and value stocks and non-investment grade corporate bonds.

The firm distinguishes itself through its specialized approach to both equity and fixed income portfolio management, serving diverse client types from institutional investors to private funds across multiple geographic regions.

_meta.type_rationale: Asset manager/broker — DNI prioritised; standard CF metrics unreliable
_meta.method_explanation: Asset managers and brokers earn fees for managing other people's money. Their balance sheets hold client assets that do not belong to the firm, which makes standard balance sheet metrics like Price-to-Book misleading. Instead, we focus on net income and revenue-based methods, which better reflect the underlying fee-earning power of the business.

company_profile.name: Aon plc
company_profile.type_rationale: Insurance company — DNI (Net Income DCF) prioritised; ROIC excluded
company_profile.description: Aon plc operates as a professional services firm in the United States, rest of the Americas, the United Kingdom, Ireland, rest of Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the Asia Pacific. It operates through Risk Capital and Human Capital segments. The company offers commercial risk solutions comprising retail and insurance brokerage, specialty solutions, global risk consulting, captives management, and affinity programs; health solutions, such as consulting and brokerage, consumer benefits, and talent advisory services; and wealth solutions, including retirement consulting and investments. It also provides treaty and facultative reinsurance; strategy and technology group solutions; insurance-linked securities, capital raising, strategic advice, restructuring, and merger and acquisition services; and risk management products and solutions, capital market solutions, and corporate finance advisory services. In addition, the company offers strategic design advice and actuarial services; pension risk transfer and integrated pension administration; and investment advisory services on developing and maintaining investment programs across various plan types, including defined benefit plans, defined contribution plans, master trusts, and pooled employer plans for corporations, public pensions, endowments, and foundations. Aon plc was incorporated in 1979 and is headquartered in Dublin, Ireland.
company_profile.description_summary:
Aon operates as a global professional services firm offering risk, human capital, and wealth solutions. Its main revenue sources include commercial risk brokerage and consulting, health and benefits advisory services, retirement consulting, reinsurance services, and merger and acquisition advisory.

The company distinguishes itself through comprehensive integrated offerings spanning risk management, employee benefits, pension administration, and investment advisory across corporate, public pension, and institutional clients worldwide.

_meta.type_rationale: Insurance company — DNI (Net Income DCF) prioritised; ROIC excluded
_meta.method_explanation: Insurance companies collect premiums upfront and pay out claims later — sometimes years later. This makes their cash flow very lumpy and hard to predict, so we rely on net income instead. The key question for an insurer is whether they are pricing their policies correctly and managing their investment portfolio well. Price-to-Book and net income DCF are the most reliable ways to assess this.

quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Aon's exceptional profitability, pricing power, and predictable earnings reflect a durable competitive moat rooted in client switching costs and specialized expertise, though modest growth and ROIC relative to ROE suggest the moat is real but not impenetrable.

metric_assessments.profitability: A 22.5% profit margin is exceptional—most financial services companies operate in the 10–15% range—which signals Aon converts revenue into earnings far more efficiently than peers. This reflects strong pricing power and operational leverage in its insurance brokerage and consulting business, where clients rely on expert advice and are reluctant to switch providers. However, the margin is held back by competitive pressure in commoditized brokerage segments and the need to invest in technology and talent to maintain its market position.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.65 and a current ratio of 1.11, Aon carries moderate leverage typical of large, cash-generative insurers and brokers, and its liquidity position is adequate though not lavish. The company's strong earnings and cash flow provide a comfortable cushion to service this debt, and for an insurance company with predictable premium income, this level of leverage is manageable and does not pose a material risk. The score stops short of 9 or 10 because a current ratio just above 1.0 leaves less margin for error during market stress than a fortress balance sheet would offer.
metric_assessments.growth: At 6.5% annual revenue growth and an expected 11.8% EPS growth over five years, Aon is expanding faster than the broader economy but at a measured pace typical of mature, well-established financial services firms. The upside to this is that earnings per share are growing more than twice as fast as revenues, driven by operational efficiency and share buybacks, which rewards shareholders even as top-line growth stays moderate. The constraint is that organic growth in insurance brokerage and consulting markets is inherently limited by industry maturity; further acceleration would likely require aggressive acquisitions, which carry integration risk and may not create shareholder value.
metric_assessments.predictability: A perfect 10/10 score reflects Aon's stable, recurring revenue model—insurance and benefits consulting clients renew policies and contracts year after year with minimal disruption—and its consistent ability to convert those revenues into profits. Client relationships in insurance brokerage and consulting are sticky because switching providers is costly and disruptive to risk management; this predictability is bolstered by the company's 10/10 profitability score, which shows it reliably delivers the same profit margins quarter after quarter. There is no material weakness here; the only reason it is not a defining strength in absolute terms is that no business is immune to recession or major regulatory change, but Aon's position is as defensive as the sector gets.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a projected EPS growth rate of 11.8% and a strong quality score of 85, Aon appears reasonably priced rather than a deep bargain; the 6/10 valuation score suggests the market is pricing in much of the company's quality and predictability. The company is neither undervalued nor overextended, which is appropriate for a large-cap, low-growth financial services stock with rock-solid fundamentals. To offer a clearer picture for beginners: if you buy Aon, you are paying a fair price for reliability and income, not getting a steal, which means returns will likely be modest unless growth accelerates or the business becomes even more profitable.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Aon is rated Safe because it combines exceptional profitability and predictability (10/10 scores) with a strong balance sheet, creating a low-risk, dependable business. The main constraint is moderate growth and a valuation that reflects its quality, meaning investors should expect stable, steady returns rather than excitement or rapid wealth creation. For a beginner, this is the kind of company you hold for decades without losing sleep, even if it doesn't make you rich overnight.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Aon is a high-quality insurance broker and consultant that earns excellent returns on its business—a 46% return on equity and 22.5% profit margins are rare and reflect powerful pricing power and sticky client relationships—while delivering predictable earnings you can count on year after year. The main trade-off is that growth is moderate (6–7% on revenues, though faster on earnings), and the stock is already priced fairly by the market, so you are not getting a bargain; this also means there is limited room for multiple expansion to drive returns. Leverage is reasonable for the industry, and the company's cash generation easily covers debt service, so financial stress is not a concern. Over the next five years, earnings are expected to grow at nearly 12% annually, driven more by efficiency and buybacks than by new business, which is sustainable but not explosive. For a beginner seeking a low-drama, high-quality holding that will compound steadily and safely, Aon is a solid choice—just don't expect it to double in a few years.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Aon plc demonstrates strong fundamental quality and scores highly across profitability, financial strength, and predictability, supported by a 13.89% ROIC.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: The risk profile is relatively contained with no major red flags across leverage, liquidity, or earnings consistency.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: With 11.8% consensus EPS growth forecast, the growth outlook supports the investment case. The stock trades at a 34% discount to estimated intrinsic value and is suitable for quality-focused long-term investors.

company_profile.name: A. O. Smith Corporation
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: A. O. Smith Corporation manufactures and markets residential and commercial gas and electric water heaters, boilers, heat pumps, tanks, and water treatment products in North America, China, Europe, and India. The company offers water heaters for residences, restaurants, hotels, office buildings, laundries, car washes, and small businesses; boilers for hospitals, schools, hotels, homes, apartments, and condominiums, and other large commercial buildings; and water treatment products comprising point-of-entry water softeners, well water solutions, and whole-home water filtration products, and point-of-use carbon and reverse osmosis products for residences, restaurants, hotels, and offices. It also provides commercial water treatment and filtration products; expansion tanks, commercial solar water heating systems, swimming pool and spa heaters, and related products and parts; and electric wall-hung, gas tankless, combi-boiler, and heat pump and solar water heaters. The company offers its products under the A. O. Smith, State, Lochinvar, Hague, Water-Right, Master Water, Atlantic Filter, Impact, and Water Tec brands. It distributes its products through independent wholesale plumbing distributors, as well as to retail channels consisting of hardware and home center chains, and manufacturer representative firms, as well as offers Aquasana branded products directly to consumers through e-commerce channels; and A. O. Smith branded water treatment products through dealer network and Amazon. A. O. Smith Corporation was founded in 1874 and is headquartered in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.
company_profile.description_summary:
Manufactures and markets residential and commercial water heating and treatment products globally, including gas and electric water heaters, boilers, heat pumps, and water filtration systems. Generates revenue through wholesale distribution channels, retail partnerships, and direct-to-consumer e-commerce sales across multiple brands.

The company operates under eight established brands including A. O. Smith and State, serving diverse customer segments from individual households to large commercial facilities like hospitals and hotels across North America, China, Europe, and India.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: A.O. Smith's exceptional returns on capital and profit margins demonstrate durable competitive advantages, though moderate growth and predictability scores suggest its moat is tied to operational excellence rather than unassailable market position.

metric_assessments.profitability: The company's 13.8% profit margin and 28.27% return on equity are both exceptional—well above the 8-12% and 15-18% ranges typical of industrial manufacturers—showing it extracts far more cash from each dollar of sales and shareholder investment than peers. This reflects strong pricing power and cost discipline in a capital-intensive business. However, the score stops short of perfect because the company's revenue actually contracted 1.9% year-over-year, indicating profitability is being defended through margin expansion rather than top-line growth, which is a less sustainable foundation.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: With a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.83 and a current ratio of 1.5, A.O. Smith maintains a conservative balance sheet typical of industrial companies that can weather economic downturns. The 0.83 leverage multiple is well-controlled—below 1.0 is considered low-risk in this sector—and the liquidity position provides a comfortable cushion for operations and obligations. The company is not stretched financially, but it also isn't aggressively using leverage to amplify returns, which mildly caps the score below a 9.
metric_assessments.growth: The company's projected earnings-per-share growth of 5.1% over the next five years is respectable but unspectacular—typical of a mature, steady business rather than an expanding one. This aligns with the negative revenue trend and suggests A.O. Smith is not in a market with strong tailwinds or gaining share aggressively. The company is expected to grow in line with inflation and modest market demand, but not to accelerate meaningfully, which is why growth scores at a solid 6 rather than higher.
metric_assessments.predictability: The company's high profitability, stable margins, and strong returns on capital suggest a repeatable business model that generates reliable results year-to-year. However, a 1.9% revenue decline signals vulnerability to cyclical demand shifts in industrial and residential end-markets, and the modest 5.1% earnings growth outlook leaves limited visibility into inflection points. Predictability scores at 6 because the business is stable enough to model, but not immune to swings or dependent on a single competitive fortress.
metric_assessments.valuation: With a quality score of 72% and financial metrics that rank above average across profitability and strength, the stock trades at a fair valuation rather than a discount—the 7/10 score reflects a company priced reasonably for its quality, not cheaply. An investor buying A.O. Smith today is paying a fair price for predictable, profitable operations, but should not expect a margin-of-safety bargain. The valuation is appropriate given the growth constraints and revenue headwinds.

company_profile.name: Algonquin Power & Utilities Corp.
company_profile.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
company_profile.description: Algonquin Power & Utilities Corp. operates in the power and utility industries. It owns and operates a portfolio of regulated electric, water distribution and wastewater collection, and natural gas utility systems and transmission operations. As of December 31, 2025, it operated a portfolio of regulated utility systems in the United States, Canada, Bermuda, and Chile, serving approximately 1,272,000 customer connections. Its regulated electrical distribution utility systems and related transmission and generation assets are located in the states of Arkansas, California, Kansas, Missouri, Nevada, New Hampshire, and Oklahoma, as well as in Bermuda with approximately 311,000 electric customer connections. Its regulated water distribution and wastewater utility systems are located in the states of Arizona, Arkansas, California, Illinois, Missouri, New York, and Texas, as well as in Chile with approximately 583,000 customer connections. It's regulated natural gas distribution utility systems are located in the states of Georgia, Illinois, Iowa, Massachusetts, Missouri, New Hampshire, and New York; and in the Canadian province of New Brunswick with approximately 378,000 natural gas customer connections. It also owns and operates generating assets with a gross capacity of approximately 2.0 gigawatt (GW) and has investments in generating assets with approximately 0.3 GW of net generation capacity. It generates and sells hydroelectric energy in Canada, and capacity and renewable attributes are produced by its portfolio of 14 hydroelectric power generation facilities located in the provinces of Alberta, Ontario, New Brunswick, and Quebec. As of December 31, 2025, it had a combined gross generating capacity of approximately 112 megawatts (MW) and a combined net generating capacity of approximately 105 MW. The company was formerly known as Traduction Militech Translation Inc. in October 2009. The company was incorporated in 1988 and is headquartered in Oakville, Canada.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company operates regulated electric, water, and natural gas utility systems across North America and Chile, serving approximately 1.3 million customer connections. Revenue derives from distributing electricity, water, and natural gas to residential and commercial customers, along with hydroelectric power generation and sales in Canada.

The company distinguishes itself through geographic diversification across multiple U.S. states, Canada, Bermuda, and Chile, combined with owned generating assets totaling approximately 2.3 gigawatts of capacity, including 14 hydroelectric facilities.

_meta.type_rationale: Regulated utility — P/E and EV/EBITDA prioritised; high debt is structural
_meta.method_explanation: Utilities like electricity and water companies operate under government regulation, which means their prices and profits are capped but also very stable. They tend to carry a lot of debt because building infrastructure is expensive — but this is normal and expected for the industry. Because earnings are so predictable, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and EV/EBITDA work well. DCF is less useful here because regulated utilities have limited ability to grow beyond what regulators allow.

company_profile.description_summary:

The company operates substantial acreage in the Appalachian Basin and Upper Devonian Shale, with significant gathering and compression systems comprising over 730 miles of gas pipelines supporting its operations.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

_meta.type_rationale: Real estate investment trust — P/NAV (P/B) prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) own properties and are legally required to pay out most of their income as dividends. This means they rarely have much free cash flow left over, so DCF models give misleading results. Instead, we use Price-to-Book (P/B) to estimate the value of the underlying properties, and EV/EBITDA to compare the business to similar property companies.

The company distinguishes itself by serving small-to-medium sized commercial clients across multiple jurisdictions including the US, Europe, Canada, Australia, and the UK through an integrated technology-enabled exchange model.
